channel.title: Agambenian Biopolitics on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Agambenian Biopolitics on emsenn.net

item.title: Bare Life
item.description:
Bare life (nuda vita) is Giorgio Agamben’s term for human existence stripped to its biological minimum — life that is included in the political order only by being excluded from it. Agamben develops the concept in Homo Sacer: Sovereign Power and Bare Life (1995; English translation 1998), drawing on Foucault’s biopolitics and Carl Schmitt’s theory of sovereignty.

The homo sacer is a figure from archaic Roman law: a person who could be killed by anyone without the killing counting as murder, yet could not be sacrificed in religious ritual. This double exclusion — from both human law and divine law — produces a life that has no standing in either order. The homo sacer exists in a zone of indistinction: neither fully inside political life (where killing is regulated by law) nor fully outside it (where killing has no political meaning). Agamben argues that this figure is not an archaic curiosity but the hidden foundation of modern sovereignty. The sovereign is the one who decides which lives count as political (citizens with rights) and which are reduced to bare life (the refugee, the detainee, the person on life support).
